Another day? My cot has been moved to the captain's cabin. He's sitting with a book. I don't know how long I've been awake. I frame a question, which emerges from my lips as a deep groan. Walton drops his book and hurries to my side.
'How are you feeling now?'
'Are we under way?' It seems uncivil, but I am having to ration out my words, each one torture to my cracked lips. Again he looks evasive. I remember now. 'Going north?' I press him. 'Must go north.'
'We're still ice-locked,' he says. 'My purpose was to reach the northern Pacific through the seas that surround the pole, and thereby to study the phenomenon of the aurora borealis at close quarters.'
'Was?'
'The men aren't happy about going on. It's unseasonably cold for the time of year, and they fear that worse weather is ahead.'
'I need to go north.' A little brandy is making it easier to talk. 'There is one whom I pursue. If you are going south, I'll trust myself to the mercy of the weather rather than abandon the chase.'
'Another man, out on the ice?'
His tone - he knows something.

xoptx Question him. He may have seen the monster.

xoptx Rest. Save your strength for now.

'You've seen someone else besides me?'
He nods. 'A few days back. It was the day before we came across you. We found ourselves surrounded by ice, which closed in around the ship on all sides scarcely leaving her the sea room in which she floated. Our situation was perilous. If the temperature dropped further and the whole sea froze, it could crush the hull. To make matters worse, a thick fog descended. We lay to, hoping for a change in the weather. About two o'clock, the mist cleared away. Stretched out in every direction we saw unending plains of ice. The crew were daunted, and I strove to instil confidence although I too thought our situation very precarious. Then a strange sight attracted our attention. It was a sledge, no more than half a mile off, driven by a man of gigantic stature. I trained my telescope and watched him continue driving his team north, ignoring all our signals, until he was lost among the ice crags.'
'Did you not find that astonishing?'
'Of course. Our reckoning shows us hundreds of miles from the nearest land. I plotted his course on our charts but, shut in by ice, we could not follow. Soon after, we heard the ground sea, and before night the ice broke, freeing the ship. But at night there was a risk of running into the loose ice floes, so I gave the order to lie to till morning. And that's when we found you. At first I thought you were the same man, but he was a giant, as I say.'

xoptx Ask Walton if he will give you a boat.

xoptx 'I need to get up on deck and keep watch.'

xoptx You can't do anything for the time being.

'He mustn't get away. And my dogs are all dead...'
'You're in no state to go anywhere, Mr Frankenstein.'
I'm surprised, but I realize I must have told him my name during the intervals when I was passing in and out of consciousness. In which case he is being very patient - we have probably had this conversation before.
'I'm stronger now. And I am a good sailor. Give me one of your boats and I'll manage.'
'You're lucky to be alive, man!'
'I dread death, true, but only because my task is unfulfilled.'
He shakes his head. 'There's no sense in discussing it. The ice floes are too treacherous for any boat at the moment. And perhaps I can yet prevail on the crew to continue in the voyage for which they signed up - though the chance of running across your friend is slight.'
'My friend?' I laugh, even though it racks my body with coughing.

Rest for now. Try to recover your strength.

'You should remain in this cabin. The doctor tells me you are far too weak to sustain the rawness of the atmosphere - as indeed I can see for myself. But I promise to put a man on watch and, if your friend should return, I'll let you know at once.'
'My friend?' I laugh, even though it racks my body with coughing.

Rest for now. Try to recover your strength.

Another day. Walton has just risen himself, and asks me how I am.
'Better. At least it feels as though I slept, rather than simply passing out.'
He looks at me, obviously assessing my health, and puts another lump of coal in the small cabin stove. Seeing me observe his hesitation, he says, 'We've used more coal than I expected. I'm having to ration what's left - though it must get warmer, surely. It's nearly June.'
'I am no expert in such matters, but in these latitudes I believe you cannot count on the seasons obeying clockwork regularity.'
'You told me you were a scientist ..?'
'Did I? It's not science that brought me here.'
'The men have a thousand questions for you. It's been all I can do to keep them out of here.'
'Do they?'
'It's quite exciting for them - a stranger, far out on the ice.' He gives me a serious look. 'They have been discontent for a while. It would help if you'd indulge them.'

xoptx Tell him you'll answer some of their questions.

xoptx Tell him you'd rather not.

'Of course. I should not have asked. You can do without being pestered by their idle curiosity, when what you need is complete rest.'
He smiles, though I can see his disappointment and the cloud of care behind his eyes.

Walton allows a group of them into the cabin. I hear them at the door babbling with excitement, but in front of me they become as shy as children.
Finally the lieutenant, who is one of the men who speaks French, ventures a question. 'Why did you come so far upon the ice in so strange a vehicle?'
'To seek one who fled from me.'
That unleashes a flood of questions. I answer them until Walton sees me flagging and ushers the men out.

'We saw him!' He repeats his statement in Russian and the others are all clamouring to speak.
'The day before we picked you up,' puts in Walton, 'we saw some dogs drawing a sledge with a man in it.'
'Where did he go?'
'North,' says the lieutenant. 'And driving like Chernobog - the devil. He'll be at the pole by now.'
They have more questions, but Walton has noticed the effect of this news on me, and in a short time bustles them out of the cabin. When we're alone, I say, 'Doubtless I've excited your curiosity, captain, and that of your crew members, but you are too considerate to press me for explanations'
'Certainly. It would indeed be very impertinent of me to trouble you with my inquisitiveness.'
'And yet you rescued me from peril. You have benevolently restored me to health. I will not hide the truth from you. I am hunting a daemon who deserves to die for his many terrible crimes. So tell me this: do you think his sledge may have been destroyed when the ice broke up?'
He has obviously already considered that. 'I cannot answer with any degree of certainty, for the ice did not break until near midnight, and the one you seek was long gone by then. He may be dead …'
His voice trails off. He doesn't think so.

xoptx Ask Walton if he will give you a boat.

xoptx You need to get up on deck and keep watch.

xoptx Rest for now. Try to recover your strength.

'He mustn't get away. And now, without my sledge...'
'You're in no state to go anywhere, Mr Frankenstein.'
'I am well enough. Purpose lends me strength. And I'm a good sailor. Give me one of your boats and I'll manage.'
'Don't you care whether you live or die?' cries Walton in alarm. He's a good man, but I have no time to explain it all to him.
'I do, but only because my task is unfulfilled.'
He shakes his head. 'There's no sense in discussing it. The ice floes are too treacherous for any boat at the moment. And perhaps I can yet prevail on the crew to continue in the voyage for which they signed up - though the chance of running across this criminal is slight.'

Rest for now. Try to recover your strength.

'Please, my dear sir - you need to remain in this cabin. The doctor tells me you are far too weak to sustain the rawness of the atmosphere - as indeed I can see for myself. But I promise to put a man on watch and, if the criminal you are hunting should return, I'll let you know at once.'

Rest for now. Try to recover your strength.

A little stronger today. I managed to get up on deck, despite the protests of Walton and the ship's doctor, and the fresh air has restored me a little. The fingertips of my right hand will have to go, but the damage was not as serious as I feared. I'll amputate them myself when I'm a little stronger - having smelt the doctor's breath, I wouldn't trust him to be sober. I will need to practice shooting left-handed.
There is a dense fog around the ship, and the ice floes are closing in. I see few of the men on deck - just shapes in the fog, though I hear whispering voices behind my back. I expect, with supplies running low, they resent my presence here. Well, I will be on my way before long - on foot, if the temperature keeps falling.
I notice some of Walton's scientific instruments mounted on deck. He evidently intends to monitor the electrical and magnetic flux from the aurora. Already I can see several ways that his investigations could be better arranged.

xoptx You should tell him.

xoptx Concentrate on your own objective.

After dinner we sit talking in his cabin. Walton thanks me for my advice on his experiments, and talks of the desire he has always felt of finding a friend who might sympathize with him and direct him by his counsel.
'You're a rare bird, then, captain. Most men are offended by advice.'
'Not I. I am self-educated, you see, and very conscious of the deficiencies in my knowledge. I wish therefore that my companion should be wiser and more experienced than myself, to confirm and support me. Nor do I believe it impossible to find a true friend.'
I see where this is leading. He is mistaken if he thinks that I am a worthy friend or mentor. 'I once had a friend,' I tell him, 'the most noble of human creatures. You have hope, you are young, and the world is before you. You have no cause for despair. But I - I have lost everything, and cannot begin life anew.'

xoptx Perhaps you should tell Walton your story.

xoptx Keep it to yourself. No need to involve him.

I keep watch a little longer, but it is so cold. I'm shaking as I return to the cabin. Perhaps my recovery is not as certain as I supposed. As I lie on the cot, I would like nothing better than to sleep, but Walton is in a restless mood and plies me with questions about my life and family.
Shall I tell him everything? It would help. Not just in practical ways.

xoptx Yes.

xoptx No.

I no longer care if I am thought mad, so I relate the entire story from the beginning. It has to be in short bursts as my fever worsens and I spend more time sleeping. Often I pass out in a faint mid-sentence, as he tells me later, but he remains by my side. Walton has the capacity of imagination rare in men today. My story has not only captivated his attention, he credits it as truth.
At last I reach the description of the village on the shore, and our pursuit across the frozen sea. 'The rest you know,' I tell him, sinking back onto my sweat-soaked sheets.
For a long time there is no sound in the cabin but the muttering of the stove and the slow creak of the hull as the ice presses against it.
'It is the strangest tale I have ever heard. And do you reflect, my friend, the kindness of fate in bringing you to one who is in sympathy with your ideas? For I believe you. I can tell by your gentle manners and admirable logic that you are no madman, but a noble philosopher who has suffered so greatly as to be destitute of every hope of consolation. And I must confess, I had gleaned a little of your tale already. In your sleep I have heard you speak to your friends – to Elizabeth and Henri and the rest – not as if they were creations of your fancy, but as real beings who visit you from the regions of a remote world.'
For some reason, his description of my delirious visions gives me a glimpse of how I must appear in others' eyes and, in a rare moment of uncontrollable self-pity, I weep.
'Oh, my friend,Walton, if you had known me as I once was you would not recognize me in this state of degradation. Despondency rarely visited my heart; a high destiny seemed to bear me on until I fell - never, never again to rise.'
'I have longed for a friend,' says Walton. 'I have sought one who would understand my vision. And behold, against my expectation, on these desert seas I have found such a one. But I fear I have gained him only to know his value and lose him. Will you not be reconciled to life?'

xoptx He doesn't understand.

xoptx He doesn't understand anything you've told him.

xoptx Ask him when the ship can continue its voyage.

xoptx Make him swear to slay the monster if you fail.

'I thank you, Walton, for your kindness to me. But when you speak of new ties and fresh affections - well, you've heard my tale, do you imagine that any can replace those who are gone? Can any man be to me as Henri was, or any woman another Elizabeth? Wherever I am their soothing voices will whisper in my ear. They are dead, and my memory of them the sole custodian of their past existence. And when I am gone, who will know that they perceived the world with joy, and had hopes of happiness and love?'
He leans closer earnestly. 'But is that not all the more reason to live? To cherish the memory of loved ones who are gone?'
'I am too weary to argue the point. Perhaps a nobler spirit than mine would forgive their murderer and draw solace from happy memory. With my mind and my education, perhaps I should launch myself on some new high undertaking. But that is not the course my life has taken. I will pursue and destroy the being to whom I gave existence, then my lot on earth will be fulfilled and I may die.'

Is there any sign of a break in the weather?

'I am reconciled to live as long as I must,' I say to Walton. 'I will pursue and destroy the being to whom I gave existence, then my lot on earth will be fulfilled and I may die.'

Is there any sign of a break in the weather?

'We are still surrounded by ice floes,' he admits. 'They have grown to looming cliffs in the week you have been telling me your story. There is no safe way through, no way back, and at any moment we may be crushed. I confess to you, my friend, what I dare not say in front of the men - we are encompassed by peril and may never see our homes and friends again. These brave fellows look to me for aid, but I have none to give.'

xoptx Ask about his family.

xoptx Reassure him that everything will be all right.

'There's only my sister Margaret. Do you know what worries me most of all, in this appalling situation? It is concern over her state of mind. She will not hear of my destruction, and will anxiously await my return. Years will pass, and she will have moments of despair, and yet be tortured by hope. Oh, my dear sister, the thought of her uncertainty and grief are more terrible to me than my own death.'
'Has she no other family?'
'A husband - and two lovely children.'
'Then trust that she will be happy, and look to your own fate.'

xoptx Are you going to reassure him?

xoptx Make him swear to hunt the monster down if you cannot.

xoptx You can only wait for a break in the weather.

'You are not the first navigator to find yourself in such a predicament. This cold is unseasonable, but as surely as the Earth orbits the sun, I tell you that the ice will withdraw. You will sail on, under the Northern Lights, make your measurements, discover the passage to the Pacific, and return to a hero's welcome.'
I can see that he is relieved - not so much by my words, I suspect, as by the opportunity to speak his fears aloud, as he could not to his crew.
'You are eloquent,' he says, a little ruefully. 'I lack that power of conviction and personality that you have even in your illness. When I hear you speak, I truly believe I could achieve great feats - that these vast mountains of ice that surround us will crack and vanish like frost from spiders' webs on a sunny morning. But I cannot communicate the same conviction to the men. Each day we are held here fills them with fear, and I dread a mutiny.'
I have my own fears. My hopes of recovery were illusory. I grow weaker by the day. But, if I am to die, can I ask Walton to take up the burden of my mission, to pursue Adom and destroy him?
the monster and destroy him?

xoptx You must.

xoptx No. It's your quest, not his.

You must hope the ice breaks up before then.

All the time I have been recounting my experiences, Walton has been taking notes. His reverent manner makes me more keenly aware that this is likely to be my deathbed.
'My strength is ebbing,' I tell him. 'For a long time out on the ice I felt as though a spirit guided me, but I see now that was mere delusion. The flesh is too weak. But the fiend cannot be allowed to continue to terrorize mankind. He must pay for the blood he has spilled. Swear to me, Walton - swear that you will hunt him down.'
Rather than give me a direct answer, he says, 'It may not be easy. You say that he is more than human. I confess that, as one who has scientific interests, I am curious as to how your process works. I mean the particulars of how you create and animate a living being.'

xoptx Why not tell him?

xoptx Better if the secret dies with you.

'Are you mad, my friend? Where would such senseless curiosity lead you? Would you also create for yourself and the world a demoniacal enemy? Has my own experience taught you nothing? Peace, peace! Learn from my misery, and do not seek to increase your own.'
I snatch the notes from his hand and shred them, though all my strength is spent in doing so. 'Fool! Would you create for yourself and the world a demoniacal enemy?'
Walton sits silent, cowed by my display of passion - and, no doubt, conscience-stricken at having provoked me to spend some of my waning strength.

xoptx Ask him when it will be safe to sail on.

xoptx Wait for a break in the weather.

Wait for a break in the weather.

'Even if I wanted to tell you, Walton, I cannot impart the details of the process in the time left to me.'
'I understand. It is too dangerous for men to know.'
I shake my head wearily. 'Superstitious nonsense. The secrets we are not meant to know are the ones God put beyond our understanding. You have been given a curious mind - the only sacrilege is not to use it. I have no doubt that another will stumble upon the same discoveries I made. My error was not in my science, but in my conscience. I was too proud. I worked alone, because I was young and wanted too much glory. I failed to act responsibly. These are not crimes against God, but against my fellow man.'

xoptx Ask him when it will be safe to sail on.

xoptx Wait for a break in the weather.

Wait for a break in the weather.

My fever is worsening. The sensation has not returned to the fingertips of my left hand, as I hoped, and I fear that gangrene may be poisoning my blood. It gets harder to go out on deck. Even the effort of conversation swiftly saps my energy.
Walton is prying, subtly. He has questions about my home, my education, my purpose in coming to the Arctic. Where are my loved ones, he asks, and in an incautious moment I tell him they are all in the grave, where I hope to join them when my mission is complete.
He is a young man, driven and a loner, and as such he is searching for a mentor. 'I have longed for a friend, Frankenstein,' he confesses to me, his face a shadow in the cold white sunlight that may be night or day. 'I have sought one who would sympathize with my vision. Behold, on these desert seas I have found such a one. But I fear I have gained him only to know his value and lose him. Will you not be reconciled to life?'

xoptx Answer him.

xoptx Ask when the ship will be able to sail on.

'I thank you, Walton, for your kindness to me. But when you speak of new ties and fresh affections - no, I am too in love with those that have gone. Wherever I am, their soothing voices whisper in my ear. They are no more, their time cruelly cut short by the devil I hunt, and my memory of them the sole custodian of their past existence. And when I am gone, who will know that they perceived the world with joy, and had hopes of happiness and love?'
He leans closer earnestly. 'But is that not all the more reason to live? To cherish the memory of loved ones who are gone?'
'I am too weary to argue the point. Perhaps a nobler spirit than mine would forgive their murderer and draw solace from happy memory. With my mind and my education, perhaps I should launch myself on some new, high undertaking. But that is not the course my life has taken. I will pursue and destroy the creature whose atrocities have brought me to this desolate clime, then my lot on earth will be fulfilled and I may die.'

Ask if there's any sign of a break in the weather.

'You are not the first navigator to find yourself in such a predicament. This cold is unseasonable, but as surely as the Earth orbits the sun, I tell you that the ice will withdraw. You will sail on, under the Northern Lights, make your measurements, discover the passage to the Pacific, and return to a hero's welcome.'
I can see that he is relieved - not so much by my words, I suspect, as by the opportunity to speak his fears aloud, as he could not to his crew.
'You are eloquent,' he says, a little ruefully. 'I lack that power of conviction and personality that you have even in your illness. When I hear you speak, I truly believe I could achieve great feats - that these vast mountains of ice that surround us will crack and vanish like frost from spiders' webs on a sunny morning. But I cannot communicate the same conviction to the men. Each day we are held here fills them with fear, and I dread a mutiny.'

You must hope the ice breaks up before that.

'We are still surrounded by ice floes,' he admits. 'They have grown to looming cliffs in the week you have been telling me your story. There is no safe way through, no way back, and at any moment we may be crushed. I confess to you, my friend, what I dare not say in front of the men - we are encompassed by peril and may never see our homes and friends again. These brave fellows look to me for aid, but I have none to give.'

xoptx Ask about his family.

xoptx Reassure him that everything will be all right.

'There's Margaret - she's my sister. Do you know what worries me most of all, in this appalling situation? It is that she will not hear any news of what befell us, and will anxiously await my return. Years will pass, and she will have moments of despair, and yet be tortured by hope. Oh, my dear sister, the thought of her uncertainty and grief are more terrible to me than my own death.'
'Has she no other family?'
'A husband - and two lovely children.'
'Then trust that she will be happy, and look to your own fate.'

xoptx Are you going to reassure him?

xoptx You can only wait for a break in the weather.

Walton and I are sitting in his cabin. We're wrapped in blankets as there is only coal dust left to go on the stove. Patches of ice have formed on the window, plunging the cabin into gloom. Walton offers me a candle but I am too listless to read.
Half drowsing, I'm brought fully awake by a hammering on the door. Walton is conversing in urgent whispers with half a dozen sailors who insist on coming in. Their leader glances at me, then addresses a long speech in Russian to Walton, who I watch slowly wilting before the vehement demands.
'What is it?' I say in French.
Walton is sitting at his desk and seems not to hear me. It's one of the sailors who replies, using the respectful tones that I've noticed they apply to me, perhaps from awe at the way I arrived in their midst.
'We been chosen as a deputation, sir, is what it is. And what we ask is only what any skipper could not in justice refuse. Being stuck in the ice this last week, most likely we won't never escape, but if heaven was to shine a bit of mercy on us, and a free passage opened up - '
'They fear I will lead them into fresh dangers,' breaks in Walton, as though roused from a stupor. 'They want me to promise that if the ice recedes we'll turn back - abandon the mission.'
'That's it in a nutshell,' agrees one of the other sailors. As an ironic emphasis to his words, the hull at that moment gives a loud creak under the intolerable pressure of the ice, and the men look around with wild eyes.
'I had not yet given up,' Walton says. He's speaking in English, but his gaze is directed not at me but towards the jutting spars of ice outside the window. 'How can I turn back now and relinquish my dream?'
'Then explain why it matters, this voyage. Inspire them.'
'I don't have the gift of authority that you possess.' He turns to me with a pleading look. 'Speak to them, Frankenstein – they respect you. Convince them we must go on.'

xoptx Do as he asks.

xoptx Advise him to turn back.

I rouse myself to address them, though it takes all of my strength. 'Are you so easily cowed by a spell of bad weather? When you signed on, didn't you recognize this as a glorious expedition? And what made it glorious? Not smooth waters and placid weather, such as sailors in the south enjoy. This is a rugged climate that demands rugged men. The voyage is glorious because it is full of dangers and terror. Because every new incident tests your courage. Because danger surrounds us, and these are dangers you have the strength and experience to overcome. For those reasons is it glorious.'
I don't know how many of them understand French, so I drag myself to my feet and shuffle over to stand behind Walton. I rest my hand on his shoulder, trying not to wince at the stab of pain that leaps up my frostbitten fingers.
'You set sail knowing that you would be acclaimed heroes, benefactors of mankind, brave men who faced death for the universal benefit of your fellows. And now, behold, the first intimation of danger. Here it is – the first real test of your courage, and you shrink away. You are content to be remembered as men who had not strength enough to endure cold and peril. And so, poor souls, they were chilly and returned to their warm firesides. Why, if that was all you hoped to achieve it could have been done with far less trouble. You need not have come this far, and dragged your captain to the shame of a defeat, merely to prove yourselves cowards. Men do that every day, in the southern seas.
'Oh, be men – or be more than men. Be steady to your purpose and firm as a rock. This ice is not made of so strong a stuff as your hearts are. It is mutable, cannot withstand you, if you say that it shall not. Do not return to your families with the stigma of disgrace marked on your brows. Return as heroes who have fought and conquered, and who know not what it is to turn their backs on the foe.'
That's it. All of my strength is used up. Seeing this, Walton makes way for me to sit in his chair. Outside, a crag of ice breaks and falls to the water with a soft boom, allowing a shaft of clear white sunlight into the cabin. The men are silent in its glare. Convinced? I don't know. But thoughtful, certainly.
Walton sends them all back to their posts. He's high-spirited now – he thinks my words have swayed them. I feel the fever eating me within. I'm dying.

Perhaps, but not yet.

'How can I tell them that? See what my own endeavours have brought me to, Walton. I had a dream, a vision for mankind, and it has blighted my life. Far from exhorting your crewmen to continue, I urge you to reconsider. Return home, find a wife, have children. Eschew ambition - live out your days as all your forbears did. In striving for a better world, man creates ruins and ash. In searching for the universe's secrets, we only succeed in unlocking the casket of woe.'
I sit a long time contemplating my own words. I am broken and feverish. When I look up, the room seems darker. The men have gone and there is no sign of Walton. The stove is cold but I am too weak to light it, and in any case the coal is used up.

I wake, still alone, and strain my ears but the only sounds are the soft groan of the hull as ice grips it, and the occasional muffled splash as icebergs split and fall into the half-congealed water.

Up on deck now. I don't know how I made it. I lean against the rail, my pulse thundering in my ears. The shrouds hang bare in a thick white fog that hides the top of the masts from view. Where is everyone? I see shadowy figures whirling in the haze, but there's no sound of voices.
Ah - I slipped. The deck is treacherous with ice. Can't get up. I'm too weak. I shout, but the sound is swallowed up. Where are they all? I start to pull myself back towards the companionway. Every inch is agonizing, my fingers feel as if the skin has been peeled away and the raw nerves are being etched with acid.

I'm back in the cabin. Did I dream that I went above? No, see the dampness of the fog on my sleeve. But if I'm awake, where are they all?
I hear a tread in the passage outside. Too heavy to be Walton or any of his crew. I'm not alone, then. Adom has come for me.He has come for me.

xoptx This is your chance to kill him.

xoptx Talk to him.

My guns - there on Walton's desk, but out of reach. Still stretched helpless on the floor, it takes all my strength to twist around as his footsteps approach. I must face my tormentor at the end, at least.
Even if my weapons were in reach, to what end? I've had enough of killing. At least it will end now.
He fills the frame of the door, having to twist his hulking torso to enter the cabin so that for a moment he resembles one of those corpses unearthed after being buried alive, their rigorous limbs pressed taut against the confines of the wood. His flesh gleams like fresh blood in the weak daylight. As he steps closer, he is able to straighten up, but even so his angular head still bumps against the cabin ceiling, and the very mass of him seems to press the air and light away. I had forgotten his size. I feel as an inhabitant of a doll's house might feel, when the wall is suddenly thrown open and a giant child intrudes its looming face and limbs and vital energy.
'The men who were aboard this ship? All gone, though I did nothing to them. A few I saw abandon ship, though I doubt their little boat will have got far before the ice crushed it. The rest have frozen and starved while you lay in your fever. Like him - '
He spins a chair. It is Walton's corpse, face dark in death. So that was how his dreams of adventure ended. I think of the devoted sister who will wait out the rest of her life with no news of his fate.
I blink away tears, but I don't know if my sorrow is for him or for myself.
AdomThe monster regards him sadly, perhaps sharing the same thoughts, then turns to me.
He bends over me. That hated face - though I cannot summon up my hatred now. At the brink of death, we would rather have any companionship than face oblivion alone.
He bends over me. That hated face that has hung before me in my dreams of vengeance. And I am helpless.
He utters a groan. 'In your murder my crimes are consummated. The miserable series of my existence is wound to its close. Oh, Frankenstein - generous and self-devoted being. What does it avail that I now ask thee to pardon me? I, who irretrievably destroyed thee by destroying all thou lovest.'
'Are those tears? Spare me your feigned remorse, hypocritical wretch.'
I can hardly make out my own voice. For a moment I am not sure that I even spoke. But he has heard me.
'It is true that I am a wretch. I have murdered the lovely and the helpless. I have strangled the innocent as they slept, and grasped to death the throat of he who never injured me or any other living thing. I have punished you, my creator, paragon of all that is worthy of love and admiration among men, with misery. I have pursued you to this irremediable ruin. No doubt you hate me, but your abhorrence cannot equal that with which I regard myself.'' "We must exasperate the almighty Victor to spend all his rage - and that must end us, that must be our cure." ' He laughs, a harsh rattle in the aristocrat's larynx I gave him. 'Well, here we are together, creator. Alone at the top of the world.'
'Alone? What has become of the crew?'
'Long gone. A few I saw abandon ship, though I doubt their little boat will have got far before the ice crushed it. The rest have frozen and starved while you lay in your fever. Like him - '
He spins a chair. It is Walton's corpse, face dark in death. So that was how his dreams of adventure ended.
'You've won, monster.' I wonder if he can hear me, my voice is so faint. 'Enjoy your triumph as you freeze to death alongside these brave men. I, at least, go into the arms of my loved ones, while you will be forever alone.'
He leans close. I think of orchards - a fruity odour on his breath. So he is starving to death. I don't think I'll mention that.
'Don't pretend to believe that you expect an afterlife,' he says. 'That benevolent God plays nicely with His toys. You created me as a spit in the eye to a senseless, uncaring universe. The oblivion that waits to enfold us is yet more illimitable than the ice that encloses us.'
I want to ask him if that's what he intends - to surrender the life I gave him, to die out here in the deserted ship. But I cannot speak or hear for the thundering in my ears. I float, and for a moment look down on the sad, misshapen giant and the small limp body beside him. I feel the nearness of that wing which no man can avoid, and darkness closes over everything.

xoptx 'And how long will this contrition last?'

xoptx 'What have you done to Walton and his crew?'

xoptx 'If that were true, you'd take your own life.'

xoptx 'I have no hate left for you, poor creature.'

Epilogue.

He presents to me his red right hand. 'Here is the instrument that executed my evil deeds. I think of the heart in which they were conceived. And I tell you, Frankenstein, I long for the moment when these sights and thoughts will haunt me no more.
'Fear not that I shall be the instrument of future mischief. My work is nearly complete. Neither yours nor any man's death is needed to accomplish what remains to be done - only my own. Some years ago, when the images this world affords first opened upon me, when I felt the cheering warmth of summer, and heard the rustling of the leaves and the chirping of the birds, and these were all to me, I should have wept to die. Now it is my only consolation. Polluted by crimes, and torn by the bitterest remorse, where can I find rest but in death?
'With wood from this vessel I will journey to the northernmost extremity of the globe. There I'll build a funeral pyre and this miserable frame you built will soon be consumed to ashes. Thus I shall ensure that nothing remains to guide the curious to create another like myself. I shall die. I shall no longer feel the agonies which now consume me, nor be the prey of feelings that can never be satisfied. You who called me into being will soon be dead, and when I am no more the very remembrance of us both will speedily vanish. We shall no longer see the sun or stars, or feel the winds play on our cheeks. Light, feeling, and sense will pass away, and in this condition must I find my happiness.'
I cannot answer him either with forgiveness or with reproach. As he has been speaking, the whiteness has grown blinding until I can see nothing else. I float, and for a moment look down on the sad, misshapen giant and the small limp body beside him. I feel the nearness of that wing which no man can avoid and darkness closes over everything.

Epilogue.

EPILOGUE

You rise from beside the body, uncertain of your feelings even now. Is it possible to love and hate at the same time? Frankenstein, your creator, is dead. You are resolved: this whole ship will become a conflagration, the funeral pyre on which he will enter whatever world exists beyond this one. You were dust of the ground and he breathed life into you, and for that he deserves exaltation.
The man can die, but the ideas need never do, for in your pocket you have his notebook still. It describes every detail of your creation. You take it out and stroke the cover, breath in the sacred aroma of ink and chemicals from the page. Such a precious text. You'll guard it well.
It is the Bible for the new race of man that is to come.You rise from beside the body, uncertain of your feelings even now. Is it possible to love and hate at the same time? Frankenstein, your creator, is dead. You are resolved: this whole ship will become a conflagration, the funeral pyre on which he will enter whatever world exists beyond this one. You were dust of the ground and he breathed life into you, and for that he deserves exaltation.
Between here and civilization lie a thousand miles of ice, but you'll survive. You are stronger and more durable than any of the old race of man. However long you have to wait, eventually another will replicate Frankenstein's discoveries.
And you will be waiting.When we are young, don't we all feel destined for greatness? I did. My feelings were deep, but that passion was coupled to a cool judgement that fitted me for illustrious achievements. Confident of the worth of my nature, I was able to persevere where others would have given up, for it would have been dereliction not to use my talents for the good of mankind. When I reflected on what I had accomplished - nothing less than the inception of a living, rational creature, I could not rank myself among the common herd.
But this feeling, which supported me at the start of my career, later served only to plunge me lower in the dust. All my speculations and hopes were as nothing and, like the archangel who aspired to omnipotence, I was chained in an eternal hell.
My imagination was vivid and my powers of analysis and application were intense. By the union of these qualities I was able to conceive of instilling life and to carry out the creation of a man. Even now, at the end, when I think of those early days when my project was still incomplete, I burn with the passion of my ideals. I trod heaven in my thoughts, exulting in my genius, transported by the vision of what I would achieve. I was ambitious, and ambition is a merciless sun. My genius proved to bear me on Icarian wings. I have had a long time to contemplate the greatness of my fall.
But here is the final darkness. The world has shrunk to a point of light. It is fading. I am fading. There is nothing else.
You hold your creator's hand. You will not long survive him, and what you now feel will be no longer felt. Soon your misery will be over. The light of your funeral pyre will fade away, your ashes will be swept into the sea by the winds, and your spirit will sleep in peace. Or, if it thinks, it will surely not think thus.
The wind and ice surround you to the limits of existence, and the frail, heatless sun looks down. But it knows nothing of the cares of human beings. Nor would it intervene to help them if it did.

This morning the ice began to move. From all around us comes a continual roar like muffled thunder as the floes split and crack in every direction. Occasionally, very close at hand, I hear the boom and splash as a boulder of ice detaches itself from the immuring crags and plummets hissing into the deeps below. We are in the most imminent peril but, as we can only remain passive, Walton sits with me in his cabin, hoping to distract himself by a conversation that, in my weakened state, is increasingly one-sided.
'The ice is breaking behind us, and with this breeze from the west I am sure that a passage to the south will soon become clear.'
I have no energy to waste on a reply, but he seems to expect none. I doze for a short while, and wake to hear a tumultuous shouting from up on deck. Walton has just come back into the cabin. As he hangs up his coat, he sees my eyes are open.
'They are cheering because they will soon return home.'
'You really mean to go back?'
'Alas, I cannot withstand their demands. I cannot lead them unwillingly to danger.'
As I hear him say this, I realize I have been expecting it for days. I feel, not passion, but the need for passion. 'Return if you will,' I tell him, 'but I will not. You may give up your purpose, but mine is assigned to me by Heaven. I am weak, but surely the spirits who assist my vengeance will endow me with sufficient strength.'
I try to rise from the bed but even that little speech has spent me up. Fingers of darkness spread over my vision.

I come to. I cannot tell how long I was unconscious, but Walton and the man who serves as the ship's doctor are both beside me. I see from the looks they exchange the truth that they will not put into words.
I raise my head feebly. 'Now
My strength has deserted me. I will die soon, and in these final hours left to me I cannot help but dwell on the thought that the monster is out there, still alive, his malignity undimmed.
It is not revenge or hatred that I speak of now, those are gone, but I feel I am justified in desiring the death of my enemy. These last few days have given me time to reflect on my conduct. I do not find it blameable - or, if it is, then a young man's lack of wisdom must be a deadly sin. In a fit of enthusiastic madness I created a rational creature, and so was responsible for his happiness and welfare. In that I failed. Later, he called upon my sense of duty to provide him with a mate - and I was weighed down by a sense of guilt. I knew I had done wrong, and I pitied him. But I had also a duty to my fellow man, and so I refused - and did right in refusing - to create a female to be his companion.
He showed unparalleled selfishness and evil. He murdered my friends. And I call that evil not because they were my friends, but because they were human beings who possessed exquisite sensations, love, and wisdom. They had not harmed him - were unaware of his existence. He destroyed them only to cause me pain. Nor do I know where his thirst for vengeance may end. Without me to torment, all mankind could become his enemy. Miserable himself, he will render others wretched. For that he ought to die. The task of his destruction was mine, but that is another duty in which I have failed.

xoptx Ask Walton again to hunt the monster down.

xoptx Ask Walton to hunt the monster down.

xoptx Don't let your final words be of killing.

Seeing my feeble gesture, he leans close to catch my dying words.
'Earlier, Walton, for selfish and vicious motives, I asked you to undertake my unfinished work. I renew this request now when I am only induced by reason. Walton, listen to me when I entreat you to carry out the mission of justice that I will not now be able to complete. I cannot ask you to renounce your country and friends to fulfil this task, and now that you are returning to England you will have little chance of meeting with my enemy. But you have heard my story. You believe me. The consideration of these pointsYou have heard my story. You believe me. The consideration of his guilt, and the decision as to where your duty lies, I leave to you. My judgement and ideas are already disturbed by the near approach of death. I must ask you to do what I think right, for I may still be misled by passion. That he should live to be an instrument of mischief disturbs me. In other respects, now
Walton must sense that I am sinking fast. He leans close. My voice is just a small gust of breath across dry lips. I don't know if he can hear me.
'Now
that I am resigned to death, this hour is the only happy one that I have enjoyed for several years. The forms of the beloved dead flit before me and I hasten to their armsOblivion spreads for me a welcome embrace. Farewell, Walton! Seek happiness in tranquillity and avoid ambition. Yet why do I say this? I have myself been blasted in my hopes, yet another may succeedLearn from my mistakes, not to eschew ambition and enterprise, but only to pursue them more wisely.'

I feel as though my friends are near. It is like the sensation of entering a room where you detect a trace of perfume, or see an empty cup, that tells you someone has just left. The smell of alpine blossom fills the air. And the sun! Instead of darkness, I see a great blaze of golden light and hear the snapping of banners on a strong, caressing breeze.
I am hovering above the scene at my own deathbed, which appears like a play in a darkened room below me, upon which I am gazing through a pane of glass. My body lies pale and straight, covered by a sheet. The stage is empty - but now a figure enters by the window, colossal and misshapen, yet somehow beautiful. I see him no longer as an ill-made man, the mistake of my youth, but a new species of being.
AdomMy creature stoops, filling the tiny cabin like an adult squeezed uncomfortably into a child's dollhouse He pulls back the sheet to gaze on my face. I look as if I'm at peace. And now the door opens. Walton stands awestruck, a posture of mere surprise that would take an actor a lifetime to study.
I could listen to them, but I feel myself being drawn away. The scent of grass, the drone of bees, like the unwinding of time back to my earliest memories. So death is like this, I think - but even thought is fading.

xoptx Wait a while to hear what they say.

xoptx Let yourself be borne away. That world no longer matters.

You're right. It was a play. While I was within it, nothing else mattered, but now all that has fallen away. The light is rushing to enfold me.

Epilogue.

The creature looks at Walton as though he were but a phenomenon of nature: a leaf that had drifted to the ground, or a drop of rain in a puddle. Turning back towards the lifeless body on the bed, he seems to tremble in the grip of uncontrollable emotion.
'So here is my last victim. In his death my crimes are consummated, and my existence is wound to its close. Oh, Frankenstein, generous and self-devoted being - what does it avail that I now ask thee to pardon me? I, who irretrievably destroyed thee by destroying all thou lovest. Alas!But Frankenstein, sole creature to whom I had attachment, is gone. He is cold, he cannot answer me.'
'Your repentance,' says Walton bravely, 'is now superfluous. If you had listened to the voice of conscience, and heeded the stings of remorse, before you had urged your diabolical vengeance to this extremity, Frankenstein would yet have lived.'
'Are you dreaming?' says the creature. 'Do you think that I am dead to agony and remorse? He suffered not a ten-thousandth portion of the anguish that has been mine. A frightful selfishness hurried me on, while my heart was poisoned with remorse. Think ye that the groans of his brother William were music to my ears? My heart was fashioned to be susceptible of love and sympathy - and, when wrenched by misery to vice and hatred, it did not endure the violence of the change without torture such as you cannot imagine.'
He falls to his knees beside the body. Walton's eyes go to the pistol on the desk, but wisely he makes no attempt to reach it.
'After the deathmurder of Clerval,' continues the creature, sobbing now, 'I returned to Switzerland heartbroken and overcome. I pitied Frankenstein. My pity amounted to horror - I abhorred myself. But when I discovered that he, the author at once of my existence and of its unspeakable torments, dared to hope for happiness - that while he accumulated wretchedness and despair upon me, he sought his own enjoyment in feelings and passions from the indulgence of which I was forever barred - then impotent envy and bitter indignation filled me with an insatiable thirst for retribution. I recollected my threat and resolved that it should be accomplished. I knew that I was preparing for myself a deadly torture - but I was the slave, not the master, of an impulse which I detested, yet could not disobey. Yet when she died - ! Nay, then I was not miserable. I had cast off all feeling, subdued all anguish, to riot in the excess of my despair. Evil thenceforth became my good. Urged thus far, I had no choice but to adapt my nature to an element that I had willingly chosen. The completion of my demoniacal design became an insatiable passion. And now it is ended. There is my last victim.''Repentance?' says the creature. 'He suffered not a ten-thousandth portion of the anguish that has been mine. Think ye that the groans of his brother William were music to my ears? My heart was fashioned to be susceptible of love and sympathy - and, when wrenched by misery to vice and hatred, it did not endure the violence of the change without torture such as you cannot imagine. Even after the deathmurder of Clerval, our enmity could have ended in an armistice. But when I discovered that he, the author at once of my existence and of its unspeakable torments, dared to hope for happiness - that while he accumulated wretchedness and despair upon me, he sought his own enjoyment in feelings and passions from the indulgence of which I was forever barred - then was I filled with an insatiable thirst for vengeance.'
'And so you slew his innocent bride. You are indeed a monster.'
'Oh, I claim no remorse for killing her. Frankenstein hated her too, you know - only I was the more honest in my passions.I had cast off all feeling by then. I exulted in the excess of my despair. Evil had become my good. The completion of my design became an insatiable passion. And now it is ended. There is my last victim.'

xoptx Stay a little longer to see how this plays out.

xoptx It no longer matters. Go to your rest.

Walton does not want for courage. Edging towards the desk where one of my pistols lies, he distracts the creature with a sweep of his hand towards my lifeless form. 'Wretch! You may well come here to whine over the desolation that you have wrought. If he whom you pretend to mourn still lived, he would still be the object of your vengeance. It is not pity that you feel. You lament only because the victim of your malignity is withdrawn from your power.'
'You're right, I am a wretch. I have murdered the lovely and the helpless. I have grasped to death the throat of she who never injured me or any other living thing. I have punished my creator, paragon of all that is worthy of love and admiration among men, with misery. I have pursued him to this irremediable ruin. There he lies, white and cold in death. No doubt you hate me, but your abhorrence cannot equal that with which I regard myself.'
'And now?' Walton brings the pistol around. The creature shows no concern.
'Fear not that I shall be the instrument of future mischief. My work is nearly complete. Some years ago, when the images this world affords first opened upon me, when I felt the cheering warmth of summer, and heard the rustling of the leaves and the chirping of the birds, and these were all to me, I should have wept to die. Now it is my only consolation. Polluted by crimes, and torn by the bitterest remorse, where can I find rest but in death? Neither yours nor any man's death is needed to accomplish what remains to be done – only my own.'
'Then where will you go?'
The creature springs to the open window. 'I shall quit your vessel on the ice-raft that brought me here. With fragments of my sledge I will journey to the northernmost extremity of the globe. There I'll build a funeral pyre and this miserable frame shall soon be consumed to ashes. Thus I shall ensure that nothing remains to guide the curious to create another like myself. I shall die. I shall no longer feel the agonies which now consume me, nor be the prey of feelings that can never be satisfied. He who called me into being is dead, and when I am no more the very remembrance of us both will speedily vanish. We shall no longer see the sun or stars, or feel the winds play on our cheeks. Light, feeling, and sense will pass away, and in this condition must I find my happiness.'
'If that were true - ' The creature steps close, and Walton is a child beside him. 'If that were true, would I not take that gun from you and wrest your head from your shoulders? But see, I let you live. You are the old race, the insects, the things that will be swept away.'
He prises the gun from Walton's hand, but gently, and tosses it into the corner of the cabin. Then, bracing himself to leap from the window, he says, 'You think me crudely made? A wretch, a demon, a monster? Those who come after me will be fashioned with more care. This is our world now. Study these remote spaces of the globe - these inhospitable wastes, these deserts and ice-fields. We may let you live in them. But your time in Eden, man, has come to an end.'
He springs from the window to the ice-raft that lies close to the vessel. I would follow him, and see where he goes and what befalls him, but my part in his life is over. The scene recedes and soon is lost in darkness and distance.

Epilogue.

'You are the one he pursued...' says Walton. 'The giant.'
'We pursued each other, both victims.'
Walton looks towards the pistol on his desk. He wisely makes no move towards it, but in coming further into the cabin he gets a closer look at the monstrous form in front of him, which gloves and fur cloak had previously concealed.
'What are you?' he says with a gasp.
'Frankenstein's shadow - or his child, or his guilt. The thing he tried to bury. What does it matter? I have hounded him to death, but while I succeeded in destroying his hopes, I did not satisfy my own desires.'
The creature springs to the window, startling Walton with his agility. I see again that he is like a great ape rather than a malformed man. There is something majestic about him. Or is that only the residue of my pride?

'I leave you,' he says, 'and in you the last of humankind whose eyes will ever behold this monstrous form. Farewell, Frankenstein! If thou wert yet alive, and yet cherished a desire of revenge against me, it would be better satiated in my life than in my destruction. But it was not so. Thou didst seek my extinction that I might not cause greater wretchedness. And if yet, in some mode unknown to me, thou hast not ceased to think and feel, thou wouldst not desire against me a vengeance greater than that which I feel. Cursed as thou wert, my agony was still superior to thine, for the bitter sting of remorse may not cease to rankle in my wounds until death shall close them for ever. But soon I shall die, and what I now feel be no longer felt. Soon these burning miseries will be extinct. I shall ascend my funeral pyre triumphantly, and exult in the agony of the torturing flames. The light of that conflagration will fade away; my ashes will be swept into the sea by the winds. My spirit will sleep in peace - or, if it thinks, it will not surely think thus.'
'I leave you,' he says, 'to carry back the mortal remains of my creator. Farewell, Frankenstein! In life I desired only your suffering, but now I forgive you your crimes against me. Your body I will see enshrined - the saint and progenitor of a new race. Or perhaps I will meet my end out here on the ice, and your species will be spared. Suffer as you did, my agony was far greater, for the bitter sting of injustice will never cease to rankle in my breast, and even had you died a hundred deaths it would burn still. And if, in some mode unknown to me, you have not ceased to think and feel, perhaps you experience a peace that I will never know. Farewell!'
He springs from the window to the ice-raft that lies close to the vessel. I would follow him, and see where he goes and what befalls him, but my part in his life is over. The image below me recedes and soon is lost in darkness and distance.

Epilogue.

EPILOGUE

Remember the tree I told you about? The one I saw blasted from existence by lightning?When I was a youth, I saw a tree blasted by lightning - completely destroyed, as though it was part of the drawing that the artist had decided to erase. At that moment I conceived the idea of the universe as a locked gate for which we, as children, hope to steal the key. That was where my mistakes began.
We start as children, but are not bound to remain so. Here is Eden - it's all around us, not a lost secret from which we are excluded. The failure to believe in the possibility of our own salvation is what traps us in the cage of the past. In ceding moral authority to distant figures, we absolve ourselves of blame and at the same time abandon responsibility for the trail of decisions that constitute our lives.
The truth is that there is almost no act, if it could but be forgiven, from which a man cannot be redeemed. Men are never monsters, though they can suffer from monstrousness. If the serpent is part of us then the divine is too, and that should warrant neither shame nor pride. What I learned too late is that it is always possible to rise above the one. Goodness is there waiting, not as forbidden fruit on the bough of a mysterious tree, but plainly in the heart.
Am I talking about myself, or Adommy creature? Or all mankind? I have reached that moment when there is no longer any distinction. The world has shrunk to an infinite point. It is fading. I am fading. There is nothing else.When we are young, don't we all feel destined for greatness? I did. My feelings were deep, but that passion was coupled to a cool judgement that fitted me for illustrious achievements. Confident of the worth of my nature, I was able to persevere where others would have given up, for it would have been dereliction not to use my talents for the good of mankind.
But this sentiment was uncoupled to the wisdom and compassion that I should have developed. I saw only the goal, not the purpose that should have lain behind it, and when I had accomplished my goal, instead of a living being that turned to me and deserved my guidance, I saw only an experiment that I wished to abort.
It was not evil, it was ambition, and ambition is a merciless sun towards which my genius bore me on Icarian wings. I have had a long time to contemplate the greatness of my fall.
But here is the final darkness. As I surrender to it, everything of my life is unwrapped and cast away. Sparks of memory, perhaps no more than electricity and chemicals, break down into the void of unbeing. But here is the final darkness. The world has shrunk to a point of light. It is fading. I am fading. There is nothing else.When we are young, don't we all feel destined for greatness? I did. My feelings were deep, but that passion was coupled to a cool judgement that fitted me for illustrious achievements. Confident of the worth of my nature, I was able to persevere where others would have given up, for it would have been dereliction not to use my talents for the good of mankind. When I reflected on what I had accomplished - nothing less than the inception of a living, rational creature, I could not rank myself among the common herd.
But this feeling, which supported me at the start of my career, later served only to plunge me deeper in the dust. All my speculations and hopes were as nothing and, like the archangel who aspired to omnipotence, I was chained in an eternal hell.
My imagination was vivid and my powers of analysis and application were intense. By the union of these qualities I was able to conceive of instilling life and to carry out the creation of a man. Even now, at the end, when I think of those early days when my project was still incomplete, I burn with the passion of my ideals. I trod heaven in my thoughts, exulting in my genius, transported by the vision of what I would achieve. I was ambitious, and ambition is a merciless sun. My genius proved to bear me on Icarian wings. I have had a long time to contemplate the greatness of my fall.
But here is the final darkness. As I surrender to it, everything of my life is unwrapped and cast away. Sparks of memory, perhaps no more than electricity and chemicals, break down into the void of unbeing. And the single thought that hovers when my mind has all but gone is a realization that comes too late: the monster I made was myself.When we are young, don't we all feel destined for greatness? I did. My feelings were deep, but that passion was coupled to a cool judgement that fitted me for illustrious achievements. Confident of the worth of my nature, I was able to persevere where others would have given up, for it would have been dereliction not to use my talents for the good of mankind. When I reflected on what I had accomplished - nothing less than the inception of a living, rational creature, I could not rank myself among the common herd.
But this feeling, which supported me at the start of my career, later served only to plunge me deeper in the dust. All my speculations and hopes were as nothing and, like the archangel who aspired to omnipotence, I was chained in an eternal hell.
My imagination was vivid and my powers of analysis and application were intense. By the union of these qualities I was able to conceive of instilling life and to carry out the creation of a man. Even now, at the end, when I think of those early days when my project was still incomplete, I burn with the passion of my ideals. I trod heaven in my thoughts, exulting in my genius, transported by the vision of what I would achieve. I was ambitious, and ambition is a merciless sun. My genius proved to bear me on Icarian wings. I have had a long time to contemplate the greatness of my fall.
But here is the final darkness. The world has shrunk to a point of light. It is fading. I am fading. There is nothing else.


THE END

Fiction interactive de Dave Morris
